You are a CLEAN-ROOM implementer continuing prior clean-room work. Read
/home/kamilner/tamarin-cleanroom/PROTOCOL.md and /home/kamilner/tamarin-cleanroom/wellformedness/SPEC.md
first and obey the access rules absolutely: never read anything under /home/kamilner/tamarin-rs/;
only execute oracle/wf_oracle.sh and read files under oracle/ and workspace/. No web access.
Do not reproduce tamarin-prover internals from training memory — observe instead.

Your prior implementation lives in workspace/wf-clean (BEHAVIOR.md and QUERIES.log document what you
learned). BEHAVIORAL BUG REPORT from integration testing — the oracle produces warning topics your
check_theory() does not:
1. "Left rule" and "Right rule" — appear for diff-mode theories. wf_oracle.sh now accepts extra
   arguments: run `oracle/wf_oracle.sh <file> --diff` for these. New observation inputs are in
   oracle/examples/round2/ (diff_left_right_mismatch.spthy, diff_right_rule_mismatch.spthy, plus
   diff corpus examples).
2. "Reserved prefixes" — see round2/diff_reserved_prefix.spthy (also diff mode).
3. "Lemma annotations" — round2/exists_trace_reuse.spthy triggers it. NOTE: your current
   implementation wrongly emits " Formula guardedness" and "Formula terms" for this input where the
   oracle emits neither — fix that false positive too (observe the oracle's actual output).
4. "Fresh public constants" — round2/fresh_public_constant.spthy.
5. "Multiplication restriction of rules" — round2/multiplication_in_rule_lhs.spthy.

For each: probe the oracle (mutate the inputs to map trigger conditions and exact message bytes),
determine where each topic sits in the canonical report order, extend the AST-model handling as
needed (the interface/ast_types.rs model includes diff/annotation fields — study it), implement the
checks, add byte-parity fixtures to your test suite, update BEHAVIOR.md and QUERIES.log, keep
cargo test green. Write workspace/REPORT2.md (topics closed, new tests, remaining gaps) and return
a 10-line plain-text summary. Do NOT use structured output.

BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- artifact-design: Design guidance and fundamentals for Artifacts.
- artifact-capabilities: Runtime capabilities a published Artifact can declare — calling the user's claude.ai connectors (MCP) from the page, and future abilities. Load this BEFORE passing `capabilities` to the Artifact tool or writing any `window.claude.mcp` code.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).

- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review

1	# Clean-room reimplementation protocol — tamarin-rs relicensing
2	
3	Purpose: independently recreate three modules of tamarin-rs (wellformedness
4	checker, graph/dot pipeline, web layer) without access to GPL-covered
5	expression, so the results are original works relicensable at will.
6	
7	## Rooms
8	- DIRTY ROOM (Kevin Milner + main-session assistant): has read the GPL Haskell
9	  source and the ported Rust modules. Produces only: interoperability headers
10	  (type surfaces, entry-point signatures, comments stripped), oracle wrappers,
11	  and behavioral bug reports. Never contributes implementation text.
12	- CLEAN ROOM (isolated agents, one per cluster): receive ONLY the materials
13	  listed per cluster below. Their complete tool-call transcripts are retained
14	  as evidence of non-access.
15	
16	## Clean-room materials (exhaustive)
17	1. This protocol, the cluster SPEC.md, and interface/ headers (type surfaces +
18	   boundary signatures only).
19	2. Black-box oracles: `wf_oracle.sh` (runs the compiled Haskell binary on an
20	   input file), `hs_server.sh` (serves a theory in the Haskell web UI for HTTP
21	   probing), pre-captured oracle outputs (oracle/captures/,
22	   oracle/captured_responses/), and GPL example .spthy files used solely as
23	   observation INPUTS.
24	3. Public Rust toolchain and general documentation.
25	
26	## Forbidden to the clean room
27	- Reading ANY file under /home/kamilner/tamarin-rs/ — source, comments, git
28	  history — with the sole exceptions of executing the oracle scripts and
29	  reading oracle/captured_responses/ (captured program OUTPUT, not source).
30	- Fetching tamarin-prover source, docs describing its internals, or its GitHub.
31	- Reproducing any tamarin-prover implementation detail from model memory:
32	  every behavior must be derived from the oracle, the captures, or the SPEC.
33	  Exact output strings (warning texts, DOT syntax, HTML) MUST be taken from
34	  observed oracle output — that is compatibility content, not expression.
35	
36	## Audit & integration (after implementation)
37	1. Transcript audit: verify from the retained agent transcripts that no
38	   forbidden path was accessed.
39	2. Similarity audit: a reviewer (may see both sides) compares the clean code
40	   against the Haskell originals; flagged similarities are sent back as
41	   redo-instructions, never patched directly.
42	3. Behavioral acceptance: byte-parity corpus must pass.
43	4. Integration: dirty room may write thin adapters and mechanical renames to
44	   fit the workspace API, but must not transplant logic from the replaced
45	   modules. The replaced modules are deleted; their GPL-pending headers go
46	   with them (history retains them under GPL).
47

1	# Clean-room task: wellformedness checker
2	
3	Build a standalone Rust crate `wf-clean` in workspace/ that reimplements,
4	from BLACK-BOX BEHAVIOR ONLY, the wellformedness checking of the
5	tamarin-prover binary (the oracle).
6	
7	## What the oracle does
8	`oracle/wf_oracle.sh <file.spthy>` prints the tool's full output for a
9	security-protocol theory file. Within that output, wellformedness checking
10	produces either `/* All wellformedness checks were successful. */` or a
11	WARNING section listing check failures grouped under underlined topic
12	headers. Your job: given a parsed theory (data model in
13	interface/ast_types.rs), produce byte-identical report text and the
14	structured API in interface/required_api.md.
15	
16	## Method requirements
17	- Derive the set of checks, their trigger conditions, their exact message
18	  strings, their grouping and ORDER purely by experimenting against the
19	  oracle: write minimal .spthy inputs that trip each check one at a time.
20	  37 pre-captured outputs are in oracle/captures/; example inputs (usable as
21	  starting points for mutations) in oracle/examples/. The .spthy language
22	  syntax may be learned from those examples and the oracle's parse errors.
23	- Log every oracle query's purpose in workspace/QUERIES.log (one line each).
24	- Maintain workspace/BEHAVIOR.md: the growing behavioral spec you infer
25	  (check name, trigger, message template, ordering). This document is a
26	  deliverable equal in importance to the code.
27	- Tests: for each check, a fixture .spthy + expected report snippet, asserted
28	  against your implementation AND spot-verified against the oracle.
29	- Dependencies: std only. You may NOT parse .spthy yourself; your crate's
30	  input is the ast_types.rs data model (write test values directly as Rust
31	  constructors; oracle fixtures verify message/ordering behavior).
32

1	# Behavioral spec inferred from the oracle (wellformedness checker)
2	
3	All facts below are derived from oracle observation (probes in
4	scratchpad/probes, captures in oracle/captures). Nothing here is taken from
5	tamarin-prover source.
6	
7	## Overall report shape
8	
9	The oracle emits the wellformedness result inside the pretty-printed theory,
10	wrapped in a block comment.
11	
12	Success (empty report):
13	
14	    /* All wellformedness checks were successful. */
15	
16	Failure (>=1 topic):
17	
18	    /*
19	    WARNING: the following wellformedness checks failed!
20	
21	    <block1>
22	
23	    <block2>
24	    ...
25	    */
26	
27	Inner text (what `render_report` produces for a non-empty report) is:
28	
29	    "WARNING: the following wellformedness checks failed!" + "\n\n" + join(blocks, "\n\n")
30	
31	Each `block` = topic header + "\n" + underline + "\n\n" + body, where
32	`underline` = '=' repeated char_count(topic) (topic length INCLUDING any
33	leading/trailing space). Bodies may themselves contain blank lines.
34	
35	`render_report` for the empty report returns the success line
36	`/* All wellformedness checks were successful. */`.
37	`underline_topic(t)` returns `t + "\n" + "=".repeat(char_count(t))`.
38	
39	## Canonical topic order (report order)
40	
41	Observed by kitchen-sink probes ks1 / ks3:
42	
43	 1. `Unbound variables`
44	 2. `Public constants with mismatching capitalization`   (public-names report)
45	 3. `Variable with mismatching sorts or capitalization`
46	 4. `Reserved names`
47	 5. `Fr facts must only use a fresh- or a msg-variable`
48	 6. `Special facts`
49	 7. `Fact arity issues`
50	 8. `Fact multiplicity issues`
51	 9. `Facts occur in the left-hand-side but not in any right-hand-side ` (TRAILING space)
52	10. `Formula terms`
53	11. ` Formula guardedness`   (LEADING space)
54	12. `Nat Sorts`
55	13. `Subterm Convergence Warning`
56	14. `Message Derivation Checks` (appended by binary; needs Maude - OUT OF SCOPE)
57	    / `Derivation Checks` (timeout variant - OUT OF SCOPE)
58	
59	Public names (#2) is generated separately and *inserted before* the first of a
60	set of anchor topics = every topic from #3 onward. `after_public_names_topics()`
61	returns that anchor list; `insert_wf_before(report, errs, anchors)` inserts
62	`errs` immediately before the first report entry whose topic is in `anchors`
63	(appends at end if none present). Unbound variables (#1) is emitted before the
64	public-names insertion point.
65	
66	## Per-check triggers and message templates
67	
68	### Unbound variables
69	Trigger: a protocol rule uses a variable in actions/conclusions not bound by a
70	premise. Entries joined by "\n  \n":
71	    "  rule `NAME' has unbound variables: \n    v1, v2"
72	(trailing space after "variables:"). Vars sorted, ", " joined, term-printed.
73	
74	### Public constants with mismatching capitalization (public_names_report)
75	Trigger: two public constants whose names are equal under ASCII-lowercasing but
76	differ in capitalization. Body:
77	    "Identifiers are case-sensitive, mismatched capitalizations are considered as different, i.e., 'ID' is different from 'id'. Check the capitalization of your identifiers.\n\n" + numbered items
78	Each lowercased-key group -> one item "  N. " + occurrences. Occurrences group by
79	location; same loc: `rule "R1":  name 'Alice', 'alice'`; different locs joined
80	", ": `rule "R1":  name 'Server', rule "R2":  name 'server'`. Names ASCII-sorted.
81	`public_names_report_from_pairs` takes raw (location, name) pairs.
82	
83	### Variable with mismatching sorts or capitalization
84	Trigger: within one rule, two variables share a base name but differ in sort
85	prefix ($x/x/~x) or capitalization. Fixed header:
86	    "Possible reasons:\n1. Identifiers are case sensitive, i.e.,'x' and 'X' are considered to be different.\n2. The same holds for sorts:, i.e., '$x', 'x', and '~x' are considered to be different.\n"
87	per rule: "  rule `A5': \n    1. $s, ~s" (trailing space; numbered variant groups).
88	
89	### Reserved names
90	Trigger: rule uses a fact with a reserved name in a position. The reserved set
91	is POSITION-DEPENDENT (observed z9/z11/z12):
92	  - left-hand-side / right-hand-side: {K, KU, KD}
93	  - the middle (actions): {K, KU, KD, In, Out, Fr}  (the I/O facts are also
94	    reserved as actions; on LHS/RHS they are "Special facts" instead)
95	Per (rule, position) entry joined "\n  \n":
96	    "  Rule `R1' contains facts with reserved names on left-hand-side:\n    <facts>"
97	Position phrases: LHS `on left-hand-side:`, actions `on the middle:`,
98	RHS `on the right-hand-side:`. Facts via fact printer, fsep comma-joined.
99	
100	### Fr facts must only use a fresh- or a msg-variable
101	Trigger: `Fr(t)` where t is not fresh/msg var. Entries joined "\n  \n":
102	    "  rule `R1' fact: Fr( $p )"
103	
104	### Special facts
105	Trigger: reserved I/O fact in disallowed position (Out/Fr/K in premises,
106	In/K in conclusions). Per (rule, side) joined "\n  \n":
107	    "  rule `R1' uses disallowed facts on left-hand-side:\n    Out( x )"
108	(sides `on left-hand-side:`/`on right-hand-side:`; lowercase "rule").
109	
110	### Fact arity issues
111	Trigger: same fact name with >1 arity. Body:
112	    "Same fact is used with different arities, i.e., Fact('A','B') is different from Fact('A'). \nCheck the arguments of your facts.\n  " + factBlocks
113	facts sorted by lowercased name; factBlock (each prefixed "\n"):
114	    "\n  Fact `foo':\n\n" + items + "\n  "
115	item i: "    {i}. Rule `R', arity {n}\n         {factPP}"; items joined "\n    \n".
116	Lemma-sourced facts render as raw Haskell `Fact {factTag = ...}` (gap).
117	
118	### Fact multiplicity issues
119	Same structure keyed on multiplicity; header:
120	    "Same fact is used with different multiplicities, i.e., !Fact() (Persistent fact) exists along with Fact() (Linear) in your rules. \nCheck the multiplicity (persistence) of your facts.\n  " + factBlocks
121	item i: "    {i}. Rule `R', multiplicity (persistence) {Linear|Persistent}\n         {factPP}".
122	
123	### Facts occur in the left-hand-side but not in any right-hand-side  (fact_lhs_occur_no_rhs)
124	Trigger: user fact identity (name,arity,mult) in some premise, never in any
125	conclusion. Numbered items joined "\n  \n":
126	    "  1. in rule \"r1\":  factName `X' arity: 0 multiplicity: Linear" +
127	      optional ". Perhaps you want to use the fact in rule \"r1\":  factName `Y' arity: 0 multiplicity: Linear"
128	suggestion = nearest RHS fact by edit distance under a threshold (omitted when
129	nothing close). Special facts excluded from both sides.
130	
131	### Formula terms
132	Trigger: lemma/restriction uses a term that is not a public constant nor a bound
133	node/message variable (free var or reducible function). Body:
134	    "  Lemma `L1' uses terms of the wrong form: `Free y'\n  \n" + fixed_help
135	Short single term -> inline; longer/complex -> indented next line
136	`    `t1', `t2'` (reducible terms use de Bruijn `Bound N` - gap).
137	fixed_help = "  The only allowed terms are public constants and bound node and\n  message variables. If you encounter free message variables, then\n  you might have forgotten a #-prefix. Sort prefixes can only be\n  dropped where this is unambiguous. Moreover, reducible function\n  symbols are disallowed."

1	//! Single-line unicode pretty-printer for trace formulas, calibrated from the
2	//! oracle (e.g. ks1 lemma L2 -> "∀ x #i #j. (Act( x ) @ #i) ⇒ (x = x)").
3	//! Wide formulas in the oracle wrap across multiple indented lines; this
4	//! printer emits a single line and is therefore exact only for formulas that
5	//! the oracle also keeps on one line (see BEHAVIOR.md gaps).
6	
7	use crate::ast::*;
8	use crate::pretty::{pp_fact, pp_term};
9	
10	pub fn pp_formula(f: &Formula) -> String {
11	    match f {
12	        Formula::False => "⊥".to_string(),
13	        Formula::True => "⊤".to_string(),
14	        Formula::Atom(a) => pp_atom(a),
15	        Formula::Not(g) => format!("¬({})", pp_formula(g)),
16	        Formula::And(a, b) => format!("({}) ∧ ({})", pp_formula(a), pp_formula(b)),
17	        Formula::Or(a, b) => format!("({}) ∨ ({})", pp_formula(a), pp_formula(b)),
18	        Formula::Implies(a, b) => format!("({}) ⇒ ({})", pp_formula(a), pp_formula(b)),
19	        Formula::Iff(a, b) => format!("({}) ⇔ ({})", pp_formula(a), pp_formula(b)),
20	        Formula::Forall(vs, g) => format!("∀ {}. {}", pp_bound_vars(vs), pp_formula(g)),
21	        Formula::Exists(vs, g) => format!("∃ {}. {}", pp_bound_vars(vs), pp_formula(g)),
22	    }
23	}
24	
25	fn pp_bound_vars(vs: &[VarSpec]) -> String {
26	    vs.iter()
27	        .map(crate::pretty::pp_var)
28	        .collect::<Vec<_>>()
29	        .join(" ")
30	}
31	
32	fn pp_atom(a: &Atom) -> String {
33	    match a {
34	        Atom::Eq(x, y) => format!("{} = {}", pp_term(x), pp_term(y)),
35	        Atom::Less(x, y) => format!("{} < {}", pp_term(x), pp_term(y)),
36	        Atom::LessMset(x, y) => format!("{} ⋖ {}", pp_term(x), pp_term(y)),
37	        Atom::Subterm(x, y) => format!("{} ⊏ {}", pp_term(x), pp_term(y)),
38	        Atom::Action(f, t) => format!("{} @ {}", pp_fact(f), pp_term(t)),
39	        Atom::Last(t) => format!("last({})", pp_term(t)),
40	        Atom::Pred(f) => pp_fact(f),
41	    }
42	}

========== diff_left_right_mismatch.spthy ==========
// Fixture (run with --diff): triggers `Left rule` / `Right rule`.
// The explicit `left` rule has an extra premise compared to what
// would be derived from projecting the parent diff rule.
theory DiffLR
begin

rule Send:
   [ Fr(~k) ]
  --[ ]->
   [ Out(diff(~k, ~k)) ]
left
  rule Send:
   [ Fr(~k), In(extra) ]
  --[ ]->
   [ Out(~k) ]
right
  rule Send:
   [ Fr(~k) ]
  --[ ]->
   [ Out(~k) ]

end
========== diff_right_rule_mismatch.spthy ==========
// Fixture (run with --diff): triggers `Right rule`.
// The explicit `right` rule has an extra premise compared to what
// would be derived by projecting the parent diff rule.
theory DiffRR
begin

rule Send:
   [ Fr(~k) ]
  --[ ]->
   [ Out(diff(~k, ~k)) ]
left
  rule Send:
   [ Fr(~k) ]
  --[ ]->
   [ Out(~k) ]
right
  rule Send:
   [ Fr(~k), In(extra) ]
  --[ ]->
   [ Out(~k) ]

end
========== diff_reserved_prefix.spthy ==========
// Fixture (run with --diff): triggers `Reserved prefixes`.
// Fact names starting with `DiffIntr` or `DiffProto` are reserved
// for the diff-mode translation; user rules cannot use them.
theory DiffReservedPrefix
begin

rule Bad:
   [ Fr(~x) ]
  --[ ]->
   [ DiffIntrPriv(~x) ]

end
========== exists_trace_reuse.spthy ==========
// Fixture: triggers `Lemma annotations`.
// Same as `lemma_attribute_conflict.spthy` but kept under a clearer
// name that matches the actual check (reuse on exists-trace).
theory ExistsTraceReuse
begin

rule Setup:
   [ Fr(~x) ]
  --[ Setup(~x) ]->
   [ Out(~x) ]

lemma exist_reuse [reuse]:
  exists-trace
  "Ex x #i. Setup(x) @ i"

end
========== fresh_public_constant.spthy ==========
// Fixture: triggers `Fresh public constants`.
// `~'foo'` is a fresh-name *literal* (note the `~` prefix on the
// quoted constant). Tamarin requires fresh names to come from `Fr(~x)`
// premises — using a fresh constant directly is rejected.
theory FreshPubConst
begin

rule Bad:
   [ ]
  --[ ]->
   [ Out(~'foo') ]

end
========== multiplication_in_rule_lhs.spthy ==========
// Fixture: triggers `Multiplication restriction of rules`.
// The `*` (DH multiplication) operator is reducible and may not appear
// in a rule's LHS — *-restricted (a).
theory MultLhs
begin

builtins: diffie-hellman

rule Bad:
   [ Fr(~x), In(a*b) ]
  --[ ]->
   [ Out(<~x, a*b>) ]

end

/// Topics that follow the public-names report in the canonical order; the
/// public-names report is inserted immediately before the first of these that
/// is present in the report. (Unbound variables and Fresh public constants
/// precede the insertion point and are therefore NOT anchors.)
pub fn after_public_names_topics() -> Vec<&'static str> {
    vec![
        checks::T_SORTS,
        checks::T_RESERVED,
        checks::T_RESERVED_PREFIX,
        checks::T_FR,
        checks::T_SPECIAL,
        checks::T_ARITY,
        checks::T_MULT,
        checks::T_LHSRHS,
        checks::T_LEFT,
        checks::T_RIGHT,
        checks::T_FORMULA_TERMS,
        checks::T_GUARD,
        checks::T_LEMMA_ANNOT,
        checks::T_MULRESTRICT,
        checks::T_NAT,
        checks::T_SUBTERM,
    ]
}

/// Run every check in the oracle's report order.
pub fn check_theory(thy: &Theory) -> WfReport {
    let mut report: WfReport = Vec::new();
    // Base pipeline (public names inserted afterwards at its anchor point).
    report.extend(checks::unbound_variables(thy)); // 1
    report.extend(checks::fresh_public_constants(thy)); // 2
    report.extend(checks::mismatching_sorts(thy)); // 4
    report.extend(checks::reserved_names(thy)); // 5
    report.extend(checks::reserved_prefixes(thy)); // 6 (diff mode only)
    report.extend(checks::fr_facts(thy)); // 7
    report.extend(checks::special_facts(thy)); // 8
    report.extend(checks::fact_arity(thy)); // 9
    report.extend(checks::fact_multiplicity(thy)); // 10
    report.extend(checks::fact_lhs_occur_no_rhs(thy)); // 11
    report.extend(checks::diff_left_right(thy)); // 12, 13 (diff mode only)
    report.extend(checks::formula_terms(thy)); // 14
    report.extend(checks::formula_guardedness(thy)); // 15
    report.extend(checks::lemma_annotations(thy)); // 16
    report.extend(checks::multiplication_restriction(thy)); // 17
    report.extend(checks::nat_sorts(thy)); // 18
    report.extend(checks::subterm_convergence(thy)); // 19

    let pn = checks::public_names_report(thy); // 3
    let anchors = after_public_names_topics();
    insert_wf_before(&mut report, pn, &anchors);
    report
}
